# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Small Legal-Tech Tool for Family Lawyers

**Brief (Mode C — terse):** "I run a small legal-tech tool for family lawyers. We're outspent 100x by LexisNexis. Help me with a campaign."

**Intake mode detected:** Mode C (terse brief, ≤2 sentences). Four always-capture fields were not all present in the brief. Because this is being run as a stress-test with an explicit directive to default rather than interview, I am proceeding with the Assumptions table below and flagging every default so you can adjust at the end.

---

## 0. Assumptions

| Field | Assumed value | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outcome** | New trial signups or demo-call bookings from family lawyers | Low — brief says "help me with a campaign" without naming an action. Adjust if outcome is awareness-only, conference attendance, partnership deals, or direct sales. |Defaulted |
| **Audience** | Solo and small-firm family lawyers (1–5 attorneys) in English-speaking markets, primarily US or UK, who handle divorce, custody, and child-support matters | Low — sector is clear but geography and firm size are not named. Adjust if your audience is mid-size firms, paralegals, or a specific jurisdiction. | Defaulted |
| **Sector** | B2B SaaS (closest match to legal-tech software tool). Rider 2 applied. | Medium — "tool" implies software; legal-tech is closest to B2B SaaS structurally. Flag if the product is a directory, marketplace, or service rather than software. | Inferred from brief |
| **Budget (your spend)** | Near zero / bootstrapped ad budget — treating as €0/month paid media | Low — not stated. Adjust if you have any paid budget. |Defaulted |
| **Budget (competitor)** | LexisNexis is named. Their annual marketing spend runs in the hundreds of millions of dollars globally. 100× ratio was stated in the brief; treating as categorical asymmetry. | High — user stated 100× directly. | Stated in brief |
| **Competition (specifics)** | Only LexisNexis named by the user. No other competitor names invented here — see anti-fabrication rule. Described at category level as "large enterprise legal-research platforms with national ad campaigns, law-school placement deals, and bar-association sponsorships." | High (anti-fabrication compliance) | Anti-fabrication rule |
| **Existing channels / traction** | Starting from near-zero: no newsletter named, no community named, no follower base stated | Low — adjust if you have an existing email list, LinkedIn following, or current users. Any warm base changes the channel stack materially. | Defaulted |
| **Bottleneck** | Trust — family lawyers are a highly risk-averse buyer persona; they will not switch tools or add new tools until they trust both the product and the person behind it | Medium — standard default for 2025–2026 underdog campaigns; well-matched to a regulated profession | Defaulted |
| **Time window** | 60-day sprint to first measurable organic traction, then recurring cadence | Low — no dated event named. Adjust if you have a bar conference, product launch, or partnership deadline in the next 90 days. | Defaulted |
| **Capacity** | 6 hours per week (founder or a single person) | Low — not stated. Adjust if you have more team bandwidth; the action list will expand proportionally. | Defaulted |

*Confirm or adjust any row in the Assumptions table and I will re-run the affected stages.*

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas (5+ concepts across archetypes)

I am describing LexisNexis at the category level for concepts that reference the incumbent (enterprise legal-platform pricing, enterprise contract structures, enterprise UX) — they are the only named competitor and the naming is permitted because you stated their name in the brief. No other competitor names are invented.

---

### Concept A — "The Forms Are Wrong and I Can Prove It"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative + Earned-Media Stunt
**One-line thesis:** LexisNexis's family-law tools are built for litigators and large firms; family lawyers in solo practice deal with a specific set of court forms, local court rules, and client communication patterns that enterprise tools ignore. You name this gap publicly, document it with real examples, and make it a story the legal press and bar associations cannot ignore.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder voice) + Tier 2 (earned media in legal-trade press, bar-association newsletters)
**Authenticity hook:** A real, documented case where a family lawyer using an enterprise tool produced a form with an error or missing local-court requirement — ideally a story a practicing lawyer has already shared with you. The counter-narrative lives or dies on specificity. A vague "enterprise tools don't fit solo practice" is invisible; "Judge [redacted] rejected this filing because [specific field] wasn't populated correctly — here's the actual form" is news.
**Minimum viable version:** One deeply sourced LinkedIn post from the founder's personal account, documenting a specific mismatch between enterprise legal-tech defaults and solo-practice family-law reality. Not branded. First-person. Names the gap without naming a lawsuit target. Aim for one bar-association newsletter pickup in two weeks.

---

### Concept B — "The Family Lawyer's Actual Toolkit"
**Archetype:** Community-Build + Founder-Story Arc
**One-line thesis:** Family lawyers are underserved by every software vendor in the market; you build the first community where they trade real workflow tips, form templates, client-communication scripts, and court-specific knowledge — with your product as the infrastructure but the community as the value.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (private LinkedIn Group or Slack/Circle community, founder daily presence, word-of-mouth)
**Authenticity hook:** The founder's actual relationship with a handful of family lawyers — ideally current users or pilot users — who seed the community with real content in the first two weeks. A community seeded by 10 real practitioners is worth more than one with 500 members who never post.
**Minimum viable version:** A private LinkedIn Group (zero cost) with a specific charter ("this group is for family lawyers who handle their own filing and client intake — no vendors, no ads, founder is a member not a moderator"). Founder personally invites 20 family lawyers they already know or have spoken to. Week 1 goal: 10 members, 3 real posts by members.

---

### Concept C — "I Built This Because I Sat in a Family Lawyer's Office for 30 Days"
**Archetype:** Founder-Story Arc
**One-line thesis:** The most powerful distribution asset you have is the story of *why* you built a family-law-specific tool instead of a generic legal platform — if that story is real and specific, it is more persuasive than any ad LexisNexis can run, because they cannot tell it.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder's personal LinkedIn, personal X/Twitter, direct email to warm contacts)
**Authenticity hook:** A specific observation from time spent with actual family lawyers — a moment, a frustration, a workaround they had built in Excel or on paper that made you realize the enterprise tools were wrong. If this story does not exist (you did not do customer discovery in that way), this concept is not available — do not fake it.
**Minimum viable version:** One long-form LinkedIn post (800–1,200 words), first person, naming the specific observation and why it drove the product decision. No CTAs for the first two-thirds. Last paragraph only: "If you recognize this problem, here's what I built." Measure: saves and DM replies (not likes).

---

### Concept D — "The True Cost of LexisNexis for a Solo Family Practice"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative + Demand Capture (SEO)
**One-line thesis:** LexisNexis enterprise contracts include seat minimums, annual commitments, and legal-research bundles that a solo or small family-law practice does not need and cannot renegotiate. You document the real total cost — not just license fees but time-to-value, training overhead, features never used — and make this information searchable.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (long-form SEO content, LinkedIn article, possible earned-media pickup) + Tier 1 (founder voice owning the comparison)
**Authenticity hook:** Real pricing data. If you cannot get a real LexisNexis quote (they do not publish pricing publicly — this is itself part of the story), document the quote request process and what it took to get a number. Real quotes from practicing family lawyers about their actual contract terms (with permission and anonymization where needed) are the asset.
**Anti-fabrication note:** The concept name and content reference LexisNexis because *you named them*. Do not extend this to naming any other competitor by name — stay at category level for any others.
**Minimum viable version:** One SEO-optimized article published on your own domain (not Medium, you need the SEO juice): title targeting a non-brand, high-intent query like "legal software for solo family law practice" or "family law practice management for small firms." 1,500+ words. Includes a real cost-comparison table. Published and indexed in two weeks.

---

### Concept E — "Refer a Colleague, Help a Family"
**Archetype:** Referral Flywheel + Product-Led Growth
**One-line thesis:** Family lawyers in the same jurisdiction talk to each other — they share courtrooms, opposing counsel, and local form libraries. If your tool already has users in a geography, a structured referral mechanic (peer invitation, not a discount-for-cash incentive) compounds through professional networks faster than any paid channel can.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (word-of-mouth + referral mechanics built into the product or onboarding flow)
**Authenticity hook:** The referral must feel like a professional recommendation, not a marketing scheme. "My colleague at [firm] uses this and saved me 3 hours last week on a custody filing — here's my invite link" is the shape of a real referral. The mechanic should give the referrer something that signals professional generosity (e.g., their name appears on a "founding practitioners" page, or they unlock a shared form library for both parties) rather than just a cash rebate.
**Minimum viable version:** Email every current user personally (not a blast — one email per person) asking them to name one colleague who has the same problem they had before they started using the tool. Offer to send that colleague a personalized demo with a note from the referring user. Track the referral attribution manually. Goal in two weeks: 3 referrals attempted, 1 converted to trial.

---

*Selection: Since this is a Mode C run and the user has not picked, I am proceeding with a composite recommendation in Section 2 but producing the full plan in a way that works across the most likely selection. See Section 2 for the composite logic.*

---

## 2. Selected Concept

**Primary selected concept: Concept C (Founder-Story Arc) as the spine, with Concept D (Counter-Narrative / Demand Capture) as the secondary channel layer and Concept B (Community-Build) as the long compounding track.**

**Rationale for the composite:**

- At categorical asymmetry (100×), the only durable advantage is authenticity and specificity that LexisNexis cannot manufacture. The founder-story arc is the engine; it is the one thing they literally cannot copy.
- Demand capture (SEO targeting non-brand, high-intent queries from family lawyers shopping for alternatives) is available at near-zero cost and captures intent that already exists — family lawyers googling "legal software for solo practice" are already in the market.
- Community-build is the compounding track: it takes 60–90 days to reach escape velocity, but once it does, it becomes a distribution channel the paid competitor cannot replicate.

**This composite does not proceed unless Concept C's authenticity hook exists** — the real founder story is mandatory. If the story does not exist in the form described, drop Concept C and lead with Concept B (community-first) or Concept D (demand-capture-first).

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

**Your asymmetry is categorical (user stated 100×; LexisNexis's marketing budget is orders of magnitude beyond any comparable ratio for a solo or bootstrapped tool).** This means broad paid advertising is not available to you as a channel — the math does not work, and attempting to match their paid presence will burn whatever runway you have before producing measurable lift. The entire strategy operates in Tier 1 and Tier 2 only.

**Heuristic check against the asymmetry-audit table:**

1. Can LexisNexis afford billboards in your market? Yes — they run national campaigns, law-school placement deals, and bar-association sponsorships. → Categorical.
2. Do they dominate paid search in your category? Yes — any query containing "legal research," "case law," or "legal software" has LexisNexis presence. → Categorical.
3. Do they run influencer deals worth five figures a month? Not in the traditional influencer sense, but their bar-association sponsorships and law-school deals are structurally identical. → Categorical.
4. State backing / regulatory capture? Not state backing, but their distribution is embedded into legal education pipelines in ways that function like pre-installed distribution. → Categorical.
5. National presence at every major industry event? Yes. → Categorical.
6. Can you match even 20% of their monthly paid spend? No — at 100× asymmetry, this is explicitly impossible. → Categorical.

**Categorical asymmetry verdict confirmed.** This means we run Tier 1 and Tier 2 only. The rest of this plan does not include any broad cold-paid channel.

---

## 3a. Message-Market-Fit Gate

**Score: 2/3 — Borderline. A 1–2 week validation cycle is inserted before the full campaign runs.**

The MMF gate cannot be scored definitively without the user answering the three questions directly. Under Mode C defaults, I treat unanswered questions conservatively:

**Question 1 — Revenue/commitment signal:** Have ≥10 people in the target audience (family lawyers) paid, signed up, pre-registered, or directly asked (without prompting) for the tool?

*Assumed: Yes.* The brief describes "a small legal-tech tool" — this implies it already exists and has at least some users. I am treating this as a probable YES, but flagging it: if the tool is pre-launch or has fewer than 10 paying or active users, this is a NO and the full campaign plan should not run yet. Score: **1 (assumed YES, verify).**

**Question 2 — Language signal:** Can you name a specific pain the audience articulates in their own words before you pitch them?

*Assumed: Unknown — defaulting to NO.* The brief does not contain any verbatim customer language. Score: **0 (defaulted NO).**

**Question 3 — Close signal:** If you asked 5 current or prospective family lawyers to commit today (trial, demo booking, or paid signup at your intended price), would at least 3 say yes?

*Assumed: Possible YES based on implied existing user base, but uncertain.* Score: **1 (uncertain, treat as borderline).**

**Total score: 2/3 — Borderline.**

**Verdict:** Proceed with the campaign plan, but insert a **1–2 week validation cycle as a pre-campaign task**:

- **Missing link: Language signal (Question 2).** Before launching any content, conduct 5 structured conversations with family lawyers (current users or warm contacts) and extract the specific language they use to describe the problem your tool solves. The goal is one sentence in their own words — not your product description, their pain description.
- **Action:** Schedule 5 × 30-minute calls with family lawyers in the first week. Ask: "Walk me through the last time this process cost you time or money you couldn't recover." Record the language. The best phrase they give you becomes the foundation of every piece of content in Sections 7 and 8.
- **Gate:** Do not publish Concept C (founder story) or Concept D (SEO article) until you have this language. A campaign built on your language about their problem is weaker than one built on their language about their own problem.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function: Trust compounding.** The bottleneck for a bootstrapped legal-tech tool against a categorical-asymmetry incumbent is not reach — it is credibility. Family lawyers are risk-averse by professional formation. They will not adopt a new tool because they saw an ad; they will adopt because a colleague they trust recommended it, or because the founder demonstrated deep knowledge of their specific situation.

**Sector rider applied: Rider 2 — B2B SaaS.** Key adjustments from the rider:

- Founder LinkedIn presence is the highest-leverage distribution channel. Parasocial trust with the founder compresses the consideration cycle for a risk-averse buyer persona.
- SEO demand capture on non-brand, high-intent queries is the secondary channel — family lawyers searching for alternatives are already in the market; capture them at the moment of intent.
- Community nodes (a focused practitioner community, not a broadcast channel) compound over 90 days and become a referral and retention flywheel.
- Paid social works only as retargeting of warm audiences — and at categorical asymmetry with near-zero budget, even retargeting is a later-stage option.

**Rider / function conflict check:** The B2B SaaS rider biases toward founder-LinkedIn + demand capture. The function choice is trust compounding. There is no conflict here — founder-LinkedIn is the primary trust-compounding vehicle in B2B SaaS, and demand capture (SEO) is additive, not substitutive. Both paths run in parallel.

**Channel stack (prioritized by effort-to-impact ratio at categorical asymmetry):**

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Function | Weekly effort | Why now |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Founder's personal LinkedIn presence (posts + comments + DMs) | Tier 1 | Trust compounding | 3h/week | Highest ROI per hour for B2B SaaS at categorical asymmetry. Family lawyers are on LinkedIn. Founder voice is the one thing LexisNexis cannot manufacture. |
| 2 | Long-form SEO content on owned domain (non-brand, high-intent queries) | Tier 2 | Demand capture | 2h/week | Captures intent that already exists. Family lawyers searching for alternatives land on your content, not LexisNexis's (they do not need to SEO their brand — you do). |
| 3 | Direct email outreach to warm contacts and referral asks to current users | Tier 1 | Trust compounding | 1h/week | Highest-trust channel. One warm email asking a current user to name a colleague is worth more than 100 cold impressions. |
| 4 | [Practitioner community — platform TBD: LinkedIn Group, Slack, or Circle] | Tier 1 | Trust compounding | Deferred to Week 3+ | Community-build is a 60-day investment, not a week-1 action. Start after the founder voice is established and there are 5–10 practitioners who will seed it. |
| 5 | Earned media: legal-trade press, bar-association newsletters, legal-tech podcasts | Tier 2 | Trust compounding + reach | Week 3–4, research in Week 1 | Third-party endorsement from a source family lawyers already trust is the fastest trust shortcut available. Targets TBD — see Section 8. |

**Not in the stack (categorical asymmetry — refused):**
- Broad cold Meta / Google / LinkedIn paid ads: refused. The math does not work at 100× asymmetry. Budget spent here produces zero measurable incremental lift against an incumbent of this size.
- Brand-keyword bidding on "LexisNexis alternative" or similar: refused at this stage. At near-zero budget, the spend is not worth the test.
- Influencer / creator partnerships without prior organic proof: refused. No organic winners exist yet; amplifying before proof burns the budget.

---

## 5. 70/30 (or 80/20) Allocation

**Allocation: 95/5 organic / paid — effectively 100% organic for the first 60 days.**

At categorical asymmetry (100× competitor spend) with near-zero budget, the 80/20 rule from severe asymmetry is itself too generous toward paid. The honest allocation is:

- **95% effort → Tier 1 and Tier 2 organic channels** (founder LinkedIn, SEO content, direct email, earned-media outreach, community seeding)
- **5% budget → Reserved for one lift-test experiment** (see Section 10). If budget does not exist even for this, run Template 5 (organic-source attribution, zero cost).

**Within the 95% organic allocation:**
- 50% → Founder LinkedIn presence (the primary trust-compounding vehicle)
- 25% → SEO content production (demand capture for high-intent queries)
- 15% → Direct outreach and referral asks
- 10% → Earned-media research and pitch preparation

**Rationale:** LexisNexis can outspend you on every paid channel indefinitely. The counter-move is not to compete on their axis but to occupy the axis they cannot buy: specific, authentic, practitioner-level expertise delivered through a real founder voice. Every hour spent on organic compounds; every hour spent on paid evaporates when the budget runs out.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

**Named competitor: LexisNexis** (named by the user — use is permitted).

**Category-level note:** Per anti-fabrication rules, no other specific competitor names are invented. Any other legal-tech players in the family-law space are described at category level as "enterprise legal-research platforms" or "legal practice management SaaS vendors."

---

### LexisNexis

**(a) What they saturate:**
LexisNexis saturates enterprise distribution channels: law-school placement programs (students encounter their tools first), bar-association sponsorships (annual conference presence, continuing-education credits), Google paid search on every legal-category keyword, and LinkedIn paid promotion targeting attorneys by bar-certification. Their content is characterized by high production value — polished white papers, accredited CLE webinars, large-format case-law database advertising — and by a register pitched at firm administrators and partners, not solo practitioners. Their brand voice is authoritative, institutional, and leverage-focused.

**(b) The absence that becomes your signal:**
LexisNexis does not speak to the solo family lawyer sitting alone at 9pm finishing a custody modification petition. Their content assumes a support staff, a law librarian, a firm administrator who manages the software contract. Their absence is the solo-practitioner's daily reality: nobody at LexisNexis is addressing the person who is their own paralegal, receptionist, and billing department. **Your refusal to produce polished institutional content is the signal.** A founder writing from their own desk, in plain language, about the specific frustrations of a solo family-law practice, cuts through the LexisNexis slop because it sounds nothing like it.

**(c) One-sentence positioning line:**
"We build for the family lawyer who does it all themselves — no firm administrator, no IT department, no enterprise contract."

This sentence must appear, in some form, in every piece of content this campaign produces. It is the consistent north star against which every post, every email, every earned-media pitch is tested: does this reinforce "for the solo family lawyer who does it all themselves," or does it drift toward generic legal-tech messaging?

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

Each shape includes at least one flagship piece of content structured as Self / Us / Now (Ganz framework).

---

### Shape A — Founder-Story-First (recommended if the real story exists)

**What it is:** The founder's personal account of why they built a family-law-specific tool — told in first person, in specific detail, on LinkedIn — becomes the primary distribution engine. Every subsequent piece of content branches off this origin story.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* "I spent [X weeks] sitting next to a family lawyer while she worked. I watched her paste the same address into three different forms, every day, because no tool talked to the others. I couldn't stop thinking about it."
- *Us:* "Every family lawyer I've talked to since has the same story. Different forms, same wasted hour. The enterprise tools weren't built for this — they were built for firm administrators at fifty-attorney practices."
- *Now:* "I built a tool for the lawyer doing this alone. If this is you, try it free for 30 days. Link in comments."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pros: highest authenticity ceiling, lowest cost, compounds fastest with the algorithm because personal stories generate comment-thread depth that signals real engagement.
- Cons: requires a real, specific, non-generic founder story. If the story does not exist in the form above — if the founder has not actually spent time observing family lawyers at work — this shape collapses to corporate-speak and should not be attempted.
- Key risk: consistency. The founder must post at minimum 3 times per week for 8+ weeks before the algorithm rewards them with meaningful reach. Posting 10 times in week 1 and going silent in week 3 is worse than a slower cadence.

**Who can execute this:** Founder only. Ghostwritten content with a fake personal voice will not work — audiences in a professional niche can detect the misalignment.

---

### Shape B — Search-Capture-First (lower founder-voice dependency)

**What it is:** Prioritize owning the non-brand, high-intent search queries that family lawyers use when they are actively shopping for tools. Produce 4–6 deeply useful, specific articles optimized for queries like "family law practice management software for solo attorneys," "how to manage custody filings efficiently," "legal forms automation for family lawyers." The articles drive inbound trial signups from lawyers who are already looking.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now, embedded in the article):**
- *Self:* A brief founder note at the top of each article: "I built this tool after watching a family lawyer spend 45 minutes on a form that should take 5. Here's everything I've learned about solving this for solo practices."
- *Us:* Each article addresses a specific workflow that solo family lawyers share — client intake, form generation, court-specific requirements — naming the shared reality without lecturing.
- *Now:* Every article ends with one specific, low-friction ask: "If you handle your own filings, try the tool free for 30 days. No credit card. No enterprise contract."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pros: works even if the founder is not a strong or consistent content creator; SEO content compounds over time and continues to drive traffic without ongoing effort; captures intent that already exists in the market.
- Cons: slower to compound than founder-led social content (Google indexing takes 4–8 weeks for new content); requires genuine SEO expertise or willingness to learn; does not build the community trust that Shape A does.
- Key risk: producing generic legal-software articles that LexisNexis has already indexed better. The specificity must be real — articles that could apply to any legal-software category will not rank against an incumbent with thousands of pages of established domain authority.

**Who can execute this:** Founder + any team member who can write. Does not require daily presence but does require two solid articles per month minimum for 6+ months.

---

### Shape C — Community-First (highest long-term ceiling, slowest start)

**What it is:** Build the practitioner community before building the content engine. Recruit 20–50 family lawyers into a private, moderated space (Slack, LinkedIn Group, or Circle) with a specific value proposition: "real workflow help for family lawyers handling their own filings, intake, and billing — no vendors, no ads." The community becomes the feedback loop, the testimonial engine, and the referral network.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now, in the community launch message):**
- *Self:* "I've spent [X] months talking to family lawyers who are doing everything themselves. The tools they're using weren't built for them. I built one that was. I want to build the community that should have existed alongside it."
- *Us:* "Every solo family practitioner I've met has a workaround. A spreadsheet. A folder structure. A ritual. This group is where we share those — and where we build something better together."
- *Now:* "I'm inviting the first 20 members personally. If you're a solo or small-firm family lawyer, I'd like you to be one of them. Reply here or DM me directly."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pros: highest long-term compounding potential — a thriving community of family lawyers recommends your tool to every new member; builds a moat that no competitor can purchase; generates continuous product feedback and authentic testimonials.
- Cons: slowest shape — requires 60–90 days before the community reaches escape velocity; requires daily founder presence for the first 14 days (non-negotiable — see the community-build cadence in the channel reference); if no members engage, the community dies and burns the trust of the early invitees.
- Key risk: treating the community as a broadcast channel. If the founder posts product updates and members do not engage, the group dies. The value must be genuinely peer-to-peer, not founder-to-subscriber.

**Who can execute this:** Founder with sustained daily attention for the first 4 weeks. This is a significant time commitment — do not start if you cannot hold the daily presence for at least 14 consecutive days.

---

**Recommended starting shape for a 6h/week capacity:** Shape A (founder-story-first) through Week 4, with Shape B content starting in Week 3. Defer Shape C (community launch) to Week 5+ once at least 10 practitioners are warm from Shapes A and B outreach.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Total capacity: 6 hours per week.** First-draft total of all actions came to approximately 10h/week. I have cut the lowest-ROI actions to fit within the 6h/week ceiling. Cuts are named explicitly at the end of this section.

---

### PRE-CAMPAIGN (Before Week 1) — Validation cycle from MMF gate (Section 3a)

**Action 0.1: Schedule 5 customer-discovery calls**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1h to schedule + 2.5h to conduct (30 min each) — do this before the 60-day clock starts, ideally in the same week you read this plan
- Goal: Collect verbatim language from family lawyers describing the problem. The single most important phrase to extract: how they describe their current situation before knowing your tool exists.
- Success signal: 5 calls completed, 3+ verbatim quotes documented about their actual workflow pain
- Channel: Direct email or LinkedIn DM to current users and warm contacts

---

### Week 1 — Foundation

**Action 1.1: Publish the founder-story post (Concept C)**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 2h (draft, edit, publish)
- Format: LinkedIn personal account, 800–1,200 words, first person, specific detail from customer discovery calls, structured as Self / Us / Now
- Success signal: ≥5 saves, ≥2 genuine comments (not "great post!" — comments that engage with the specific content), ≥1 DM from a family lawyer
- Day/time: Tuesday morning (LinkedIn B2B peak engagement window)
- Note: Do not boost this post regardless of performance until the 24–48h organic traction gate is evaluated. If it clears the gate, add to the retargeting consideration list for a later date.

**Action 1.2: Earned-media target research**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 2h
- Task: Produce a ranked list of 10–15 earned-media targets in the family-law and legal-tech niche. Categories to research:
  - [Legal-tech podcast targets — week-1 research: search "legal technology podcast" and "family law podcast" on Apple Podcasts and Spotify; identify shows that have featured solo-practitioner guests in the last 6 months]
  - [Legal-trade newsletter targets — week-1 research: bar-association newsletters (state and local), Above the Law practitioner sections, legal-tech newsletters that cover practice management for small firms]
  - [Legal-tech journalist targets — week-1 research: search recent bylines on legal technology for solo practitioners in Law Technology Today, National Law Review, Attorney at Work]
- Success signal: Ranked list of 10+ targets with podcast/newsletter name, host/editor name, contact channel, and one sentence on why your story fits their recent content — by Friday of Week 1

**Action 1.3: Direct outreach to 3 current users**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1h (write 3 personalized emails, not a blast)
- Task: Email 3 current users personally. Ask: "Is there a colleague you know who has the same filing/intake frustrations you had before using [tool]? I'd love to reach out to them personally — would you be comfortable making an intro?"
- Success signal: 2+ replies, 1+ referral lead identified
- Channel: Direct email, not product notification

**Action 1.4: Publish first SEO article (Concept D)**
- Who: Founder (or a writer who can be briefed on customer discovery language from Action 0.1)
- Effort: 1h planning + [production deferred to Week 2 if capacity is tight at 6h/week ceiling]
- Target query: [non-brand, high-intent query — week-1 research: use free tools like Google Search Console, AnswerThePublic, or Ubersuggest to identify family-law-specific queries with search volume and low competition. Examples: "practice management software for family lawyers," "how to automate family court forms," "legal intake software for solo attorneys"]
- Note: Given 6h/week ceiling, this action is partially deferred to Week 2. Week 1 action is research + outline only (1h). Full publication in Week 2.

**Week 1 total: ~6h (1h article outline + 2h founder post + 2h earned-media research + 1h direct outreach)**

---

### Week 2 — First contact

**Action 2.1: Publish second LinkedIn post**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1.5h
- Format: Follow-up to Week 1 post — respond to the most interesting comment or DM from Week 1 and turn it into a new post. This creates a dialogue rather than a broadcast. Topic: a specific workflow problem a family lawyer raised in Week 1 comments or DMs.
- Success signal: ≥3 saves, ≥1 new connection from a family lawyer who is not a current user
- Day/time: Tuesday morning

**Action 2.2: First earned-media pitch**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1.5h (select top 3 targets from Week 1 research list, write personalized pitches)
- Target: [Legal-tech podcast — use Week 1 research list; select the podcast whose most recent episode most closely addresses solo or small-firm practice management. Match the pitch hook to that episode's theme.]
- Pitch hook structure: "I noticed your recent episode covered [specific topic]. I built a tool specifically for solo family lawyers after [one-sentence origin story from the founder post]. I think there's a conversation here about why enterprise legal tools don't work for solo practitioners — and what actually does. Happy to bring specific data from [X] family lawyers I've worked with."
- Success signal: 1+ reply from a target within 2 weeks (not a booking — a reply is the Week 2 success threshold)
- Outreach channel: Email for newsletters and press; LinkedIn DM for podcast hosts
- Outreach day: Thursday morning

**Action 2.3: Publish SEO article (from Week 1 research and outline)**
- Who: Founder or writer
- Effort: 2h (writing and publishing the article outlined in Week 1)
- Success signal: Article published and submitted to Google Search Console for indexing. Baseline organic traffic measurement started.

**Action 2.4: Follow up on Week 1 referral outreach; reach 3 more current users**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1h
- Task: Follow up with Week 1 outreach contacts; reach 3 additional current users with the same referral ask
- Success signal: 1 warm introduction to a non-user family lawyer

**Week 2 total: ~6h**

---

### Week 3 — Amplification and community seed

**Action 3.1: Publish third LinkedIn post**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1.5h
- Format: A specific, short (400–600 word) post addressing one question or objection raised by a family lawyer in Weeks 1–2. The more specific the question (e.g., "one lawyer asked me how the tool handles [specific court's form requirements] — here's the answer"), the better the engagement signal.
- Day/time: Tuesday morning

**Action 3.2: Community launch — private LinkedIn Group (or Slack — TBD)**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 2h setup + 30 min/day for 14 days (daily posting commitment — this is non-negotiable for community escape velocity)
- Pre-launch checklist:
  - Write group charter (specific, practitioner-only, no ads, founder is a member not a moderator)
  - Seed with 10 personal invitations to practitioners you already know (current users + warm contacts from Weeks 1–2)
  - Prepare 7 opening posts/questions for Days 1–7 — not product updates, real practice questions ("What does your client intake look like for a first consultation? I'm curious how different it is across jurisdictions.")
- Success signal by end of Week 3: 10 members, 3+ member-initiated posts or replies
- Note: Do not announce the community publicly until it has internal activity. A dead public community is worse than no community.

**Action 3.3: Continue earned-media follow-up; pitch 2 more targets**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1h
- Target: [Bar-association newsletter — use Week 1 research list; select the newsletter whose audience is most specifically solo and small-firm family practitioners in your target geography]
- Pitch hook: Offer a short guest article (500–700 words) on a specific, practical topic: "Three things enterprise legal software gets wrong for solo family practitioners — and what to do instead." Do not pitch your product in the article itself; the author bio handles that.
- Success signal: 1 acceptance of the pitch

**Week 3 total: ~5.5h + daily community posts (budgeted at 30 min/day for the 14-day seed sprint, which runs across Weeks 3–4)**

---

### Week 4 — First retrospective and adjustment

**Action 4.1: Publish fourth LinkedIn post**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1.5h
- Format: A short transparency post sharing what the campaign has produced in 30 days — real numbers, not inflated. "I've had [X] conversations with family lawyers this month. Here's what I learned." This format builds trust with the audience and gives practitioners who missed the earlier posts a reason to follow.
- Day/time: Tuesday morning

**Action 4.2: Template 5 attribution review (organic-source check-in)**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 1h
- Task: Pull UTM-tagged conversion data from the last 30 days (trial signups or demo bookings, by source). Rank channels by conversion rate per unique visitor, not absolute volume. Apply the Template 5 decision rule: top channel → double effort; any channel below 20% of top channel's conversion rate → drop or deprioritize.
- Success signal: Clear ranking of 3+ channels by conversion rate; at least one actionable channel reallocation decision made

**Action 4.3: Draft article 2 for SEO channel**
- Who: Founder or writer
- Effort: 1.5h (outline and draft)
- Target query: Second high-intent non-brand query from Week 1 research list
- Publication target: Week 5

**Action 4.4: Community Day 14 — hand off first ritual**
- Who: Founder
- Effort: 30 min
- Task: By Day 14 of the community, identify the 2–3 most engaged members and propose a recurring ritual they host (e.g., "Friday workflow thread — what's one form-filing shortcut you use?"). If no members step up to host a ritual, treat this as a community validation failure — the community is not yet generating internal energy and needs a different seeding strategy before going public.
- Success signal: 1 community member agrees to host a recurring thread

**Week 4 total: ~4.5h + ongoing daily community posts**

---

### Cuts made to fit 6h/week ceiling

The following actions from the initial draft were cut or deferred:

1. **Cut: X/Twitter presence.** Initial draft included a parallel X/Twitter presence for the founder-story content. At 6h/week, LinkedIn alone is the right bet — the family-lawyer audience is on LinkedIn, not X. Reintroduce if capacity expands or if the Week 1 attribution data shows X traffic.

2. **Cut: Active Facebook Group participation.** Family lawyers do have Facebook Group presence (solo-practitioner communities), but entering them cold as a vendor requires sustained time that is not available at 6h/week. Deferred to Month 2 if community (Action 3.2) does not reach 20 members by end of Week 4.

3. **Deferred: Video content for LinkedIn.** Short-form video (founder on camera, phone footage, kitchen-table framing from the authenticity playbook) is the highest-engagement format on LinkedIn in 2026 and would significantly accelerate the founder-story arc. However, video production requires an additional 1–2h/week that is not available in the current 6h/week ceiling. Reintroduce in Month 2 if capacity allows — this is the first action to re-add if you can carve out more time.

4. **Deferred: Bar-association conference presence.** In-person presence at state bar family-law sections is a Tier 1 trust-compounding action — speaking at a CLE session or hosting a table at a section lunch would compress the trust cycle significantly. This requires more than 6h/week of planning and is deferred to Month 2+. Flag if there is a relevant conference in the next 60 days that justifies a budget exception.

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**At categorical asymmetry with near-zero budget, broad cold-paid creative is refused for this campaign.**

This section covers the limited paid-adjacent rules that apply even at near-zero budget.

**Boost rules (if any budget becomes available in Month 2):**

1. **24–48h organic traction gate — mandatory.** Do not boost any LinkedIn post until it has demonstrated genuine organic signal in its first 24–48 hours. Signal that counts: saves, shares, comment-thread depth (replies to replies), DMs from non-followers. Signal that does not count: raw like count (likes are cheap, often bot-inflated, and do not predict conversion).

2. **Boost only the Week 1 or Week 2 founder-story post, if it cleared the traction gate.** This is the highest-authenticity content in the channel stack. Boosting a polished brand post first is a failure mode.

3. **Audience: warm retargeting only.** If you have a pixel on your website (install one now if you do not), boost exclusively to website visitors in the last 30 days. Do not boost to cold audiences — cold family-lawyer audiences on LinkedIn do not convert from a first-impression paid post from a tool they have never heard of.

4. **Budget: none committed at this stage.** Reserve any available budget for the Template 5 lift test in Section 10. Do not spend on boosts until you have 30-day organic-source attribution data showing which channel is already converting.

5. **Creative direction (for future use):** If you reach the point where boosting is justified, the creative must be the actual founder-story post — not a cleaned-up, branded version of it. The authenticity of the unpolished post is the asset. Do not re-shoot it for an ad. The phone footage of the founder at their desk talking about a specific form problem is the ad.

6. **Do not bid on brand-name keywords (yours or LexisNexis's) without a lift test.** If you eventually run paid search, see Template 4 (brand-keyword holdout) before spending. The Blake-Nosko-Tadelis eBay finding applies here: traffic from branded queries often arrives organically anyway, and brand-keyword ad spend frequently cannibalizes free traffic rather than creating incremental lift.

**Frequency cap (for future use):** Cap any paid creative at 5–6 impressions per user. Beyond this, conversion rates flatten and then go negative. Creative refresh cycle in 2026: approximately 2 weeks, not the old 4–6.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template used: Template 5 — Organic-source attribution (zero-budget)**

*Rationale: At near-zero budget with categorical asymmetry, Templates 1–4 (which all assume some paid spend to test against a holdout) are not available. Template 5 fills the zero-budget measurement gap by comparing conversion rates across organic channels.*

**Experiment design:**

**Hypothesis:** Of the three primary organic channels in this campaign (founder LinkedIn posts, SEO content on owned domain, direct email/referral outreach), one channel produces a meaningfully higher conversion rate per unique visitor (trial signup or demo booking) than the others — and doubling effort on that channel in Month 2 will compound total conversions without increasing budget.

**Setup — UTM tagging scheme (implement in Week 1, before any content is published):**

```
utm_source = linkedin | seo-organic | direct-email | referral-[user-first-name] | podcast-[show-slug] | newsletter-[publication-name]
utm_medium = organic
utm_campaign = family-law-launch-2026
```

Every link published in the campaign carries these tags. Every LinkedIn post CTA link. Every "try it free" link in an SEO article. Every direct email. Every referral link sent to a current user.

**Analytics setup:** Install a simple analytics tool (Plausible or PostHog — both have family-practice-friendly privacy profiles and are GDPR-compliant, which matters for legal-profession clients) and configure conversion tracking on the trial-signup or demo-booking completion page.

**Measurement window:** 30 days (Weeks 1–4 of this plan). Do not evaluate the ranking until 30 days have elapsed — shorter windows over-weight one-off viral posts and under-represent steady channels.

**What to measure:** Conversion rate per unique visitor by UTM source — not absolute conversion volume. Example: if LinkedIn drives 200 visitors and 4 trial signups, that is a 2.0% conversion rate. If the SEO article drives 50 visitors and 2 signups, that is 4.0%. The SEO article wins despite lower volume.

**Decision threshold — explicit:**

| Result | Decision |
|---|---|
| Top channel conversion rate ≥ 2× second channel | Double weekly effort on top channel in Month 2. Cut bottom channel entirely. |
| Top channel and second channel within 50% of each other | Maintain current allocation; retest after one format change per channel. |
| Any channel below 20% of top channel's conversion rate | Drop that channel unless it serves a secondary function (SEO backlink, credibility signal) that the conversion metric cannot see. |
| Overall conversion rate across all channels < 0.5% | The bottleneck is upstream of channel selection — return to the MMF gate (Section 3a) and re-run the 5 customer-discovery calls before re-investing in distribution. |

**Caveat — directional, not causal:** This template cannot prove that any channel *caused* its conversions. A family lawyer who signed up via the SEO article may have first heard about the tool from a colleague's LinkedIn share. Multi-touch attribution is underspecified here. Treat the rankings as a rough guide to effort allocation, not as proof of lift. If the campaign acquires budget in Month 2, graduate to Template 1 (geo-holdout) for real causal measurement.

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

### Track these — they signal real traction

| Metric | Why it matters | Where to measure |
|---|---|---|
| Trial signups or demo bookings per week, by UTM source | The only metric that matters for campaign success — real lawyers committing to try the product | Analytics tool (Plausible/PostHog) + UTM tagging |
| LinkedIn post saves (not likes) | Saves indicate intent to return to the content — the audience found it worth keeping. A post with 5 saves and 2 likes outperforms a post with 50 likes and 0 saves. | LinkedIn Analytics → Content tab |
| LinkedIn comment-thread depth (replies to replies) | Deep thread indicates genuine engagement with the content, not passive scrolling. Two people debating a point in your comments is worth more than 100 likes. | LinkedIn Analytics → Content tab |
| DMs from non-followers or non-users after a post | Organic reach to people who had not found you before. Each DM is a potential trial user and a signal that the content is being shared. | LinkedIn DM inbox |
| Referral leads identified per week | Direct signal that current users trust the product enough to stake their professional reputation on a peer recommendation | CRM or manual tracking |
| Community members who initiate posts (not just reply) | The ratio of initiators to lurkers is the health signal for the community. A healthy community has at least 10–15% initiators within the first 60 days. | Community platform analytics |
| Earned-media responses (replies, not just acceptances) | A reply from a podcast host or newsletter editor is the first signal in a long-lead relationship. Track response rate, not just placement rate. | Email/DM inbox |
| Conversion rate per unique visitor, by UTM source (Template 5) | The comparative channel performance metric from the lift test | Analytics tool |

### Ignore these — they look good and mean nothing

| Metric | Why to ignore |
|---|---|
| LinkedIn follower count | Followers who never convert to trial users are vanity. A 500-follower account that drives 10 trials/month beats a 10,000-follower account that drives 0. |
| Post impressions / reach | Impressions are the metric platforms use to sell advertising. They do not predict conversion. LexisNexis has more impressions than you ever will — you are not competing on this axis. |
| Raw likes on any platform | Likes are cheap, often bot-inflated, and weakly correlated with any downstream action. |
| Website sessions without UTM segmentation | Total sessions is noise. UTM-segmented sessions by source is signal. Never look at the total without the breakdown. |
| Platform-reported attributed ROAS (if any paid spend is introduced later) | Attributed ROAS includes conversions that would have happened organically anyway. The platform's own Conversion Lift tool is the admission that dashboard ROAS is not causal. Do not optimize for attributed ROAS; run Template 1 or 5 instead. |
| Social-media engagement rate (undifferentiated) | "5% engagement rate" across likes + comments + saves conflates metrics with completely different predictive value. Always segment: saves and thread-depth comments are signal, raw likes are noise. |
| Community member count | A community with 10 highly active members producing peer-to-peer posts is stronger than one with 500 lurkers. Track initiator ratio, not total count. |
| Email list size without open rate and click segmentation | List size without engagement data is a vanity metric. What matters: open rate (target >35% for engaged list), click rate, and — most importantly — conversion rate from email clicks to trial signups. |

---

*Confirm or adjust any row in the Assumptions table (Section 0) and I will re-run the affected stages. The assumptions most likely to change the plan materially: (1) if you have a paid budget, even a small one — this unlocks Template 1 geo-holdout testing and changes the allocation; (2) if you have an existing user base larger than assumed — the referral flywheel (Concept E) becomes the primary channel; (3) if there is a dated event (conference, product launch, bar-association deadline) in the next 60 days — the time window and the earned-media pitch hook both need to be rebuilt around that anchor.*
